title: Anno interview from 1996
wikitext:
[]: Anno interview from 1996             Quote Post by Kendrix » Wed Sep 18, 2013 6:24 pm Title amended from "Have we discussed this one yet" This interview on 17th angel, I don't think it's that commonly known: It appears to be from between the finishing of the series and the release of EoE, with the Dc episodes still in production. SPOILER: Show

[]: Re: Have we discussed this one yet?             Quote Post by Monk Ed » Wed Sep 18, 2013 6:57 pm Interesting stuff there, Kendrix. But we have to do something about that topic title... (EDIT: Someone did it for me.) Also: —— What are NERV’s bathhouse staff like? They should wear uniform. Which energy source do they use? Anno: Sure enough it is nuclear energy *laugh*. The remaining hot water that nuclear reactor left behind. The secondary cooling water should do the work for the bathhouse.

[]:             Quote Post by Dream » Thu Sep 19, 2013 2:44 am Anno: YEAH

[]:             Quote Post by Monk Ed » Thu Sep 19, 2013 3:09 am Indeed, there's a lot to discuss in this interview. It seems impossible to do so without covering many topics at once, and I can't imagine it will be feasible to ask everyone to create a whole new thread for each topic they want to address, quoting from and linking back to the original article. I'm going to ask for that anyway . Who knows, it might work.

[]:             Quote Post by Monk Ed » Thu Sep 19, 2013 4:38 am Janitorial note: All replies to this: Kendrix wrote: Oh, another interesting bit i stumbled over in that same googling session is that, apparently, Shinji was supposed to have lines after Kaworu's death (In Q) , but Ogata couldn't bring herself to say them, so Anno impromptu decided to cut em all, probably for realism. Noe I'm kinda curious as to what they would have been.

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Thu Sep 19, 2013 6:46 am Anno: Why would you think the theme is “you can’t run away”?

[]:             Quote Post by Kendrix » Sat Sep 21, 2013 2:32 pm NemZ wrote: Awesome. Now apply that to instrumentality and see that rejecting it outright isn't necessarily correct.
